note: 30 representative units of the 2,500-row TechCorp exit survey. Paged unit listings in fixtures mode serve from this set; counts elsewhere honestly describe the full corpus.

units.text: I stayed three years longer than I should have. The pay was fine, honestly — it was watching every good manager leave while the broken processes stayed. By the end I was doing two jobs and interviewing for a third.
El salario nunca alcanzó y la gerencia jamás escuchaba; aun así, voy a extrañar muchísimo a mi equipo.
My director ⟨NAME_1⟩ promised a promotion for two cycles running. I believed ⟨NAME_1⟩ both times. That is on me.
Oh sure, the workload was totally reasonable. Eighty-hour weeks build character, right?
Base salary sat 18% under market for my level and the refresh grants never came. I gave finance two comp cycles to fix it. They didn't, so a competitor did.
Loved the people. Left for money. It really is that simple.
Quota went up 40% in January with no territory change and commission accelerators got quietly capped. When the top three reps all leave in one quarter, the comp plan is the message.
My manager was the single best thing about this company. When she left in March there was nobody steering, and the new org chart had me reporting to a committee.

Four reorgs in eighteen months. Each time leadership promised clarity and each time we got a new layer of directors who had never met a customer. I stopped believing the all-hands slides about a year ago.
On-call every third week for a year. I did the math at my doctor's office, which tells you everything.
Burned out, plainly. We shipped the migration with half the promised headcount and then the 'thank you' was a bigger roadmap. I have nothing left to give and that scares me more than leaving does.
Support queue doubled, headcount didn't. Management calls it 'doing more with less.' I call it why my best people and now I are gone.
Three years, zero promotions, two 'exceeds expectations' reviews. The growth conversations were always next quarter's conversation.
I asked for a development plan three times. What I got was a license to a learning platform and a manager who booked our careers chat over lunch and then ate through it.
The return-to-office mandate was the end for me. I was hired remote, I built my life remote, and the badge-swipe dashboards made it clear trust had left the building before I did.
Mandating three days in an office two hours from my house is just a layoff with extra steps.
Honestly? Great run. I learned more here in four years than in the previous ten. The new role is a step up we both knew this place couldn't offer yet.
La política de regreso a la oficina no consideró a los equipos de LATAM. Tres días presenciales en una oficina que ni siquiera existe en mi país.
asdf
Pay was fair, manager was kind, I just moved cities for family. Nothing here pushed me out.
I regret leaving already, honestly. Signed elsewhere during the hiring freeze panic and the counter came two days too late. If the door reopens at the right level, call me.
Middle management here is a weather system: unpredictable, mostly noise, occasionally destructive. My skip-level never learned my name in two years.
The work itself was the best of my career. The pace was not survivable. Both things are true and the second one wins.
Underpaid relative to the new hires doing my job with my training. HR confirmed the band compression in writing and then scheduled a meeting about scheduling a meeting.
n/a
Two managers in one year, neither of whom set a single clear priority. I wrote my own goals, graded my own homework, and eventually hired my own replacement employer.
Loved the people. Left for money. It really is that simple.
Compensation review was 'paused for macro conditions' while we sponsored a stadium. People notice. People do math. People leave.
I wanted to grow into architecture work. There was no path, no ladder, not even a wall to climb. Every senior role went to an external hire with a worse track record and a better network.
Weekend releases became weekly releases became 'we own the pager now.' My kids started asking why the laptop comes on vacation. That question ended eleven years here.
